name: dataframe
context: Pandas DataFrame mental model: Series, Index, dtypes, missing values, selection, assignment, and vectorized operations
info: A pandas DataFrame is the central labeled table abstraction for Python data analysis. Agents need this node before writing transformations because most correctness bugs come from dtype inference, row/column alignment, chained assignment, missing-value semantics, and using slow row loops instead of vectorized operations. This context explains the DataFrame, Series, and Index model, shows safe `.loc` and `.iloc` access, describes nullable dtypes and missing markers, and points to [[pandas/workflows/clean]], [[pandas/workflows/transform]], [[pandas/workflows/groupby]], and [[pandas/workflows/performance]] for applied work.
triggers: why is pandas aligning data by index
use loc instead of chained assignment
select rows and columns in pandas
body:
# DataFrame Mental Model

A DataFrame is a labeled two-dimensional table. Columns are `Series`, rows are addressed by an `Index`, and operations align on labels. This label alignment is powerful but can surprise agents that expect positional arrays.

## Inspect first

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('orders.csv')
print(df.shape)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.info())
print(df.head())
print(df.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False))
```

## Selection

```python
paid = df.loc[df['status'].eq('paid'), ['order_id', 'amount', 'created_at']]
third_row = df.iloc[2]
amount = df.at[0, 'amount']
```

Use `.loc` for label-based row and column selection, `.iloc` for position-based selection, `.at` and `.iat` for scalar access. Avoid chained assignment like `df[df.status == 'paid']['amount'] = 0`; use `df.loc[mask, 'amount'] = 0`.

## Dtypes and missing values

Use `convert_dtypes()` after messy imports, then repair important columns explicitly. Nullable dtypes such as `string`, `Int64`, and `boolean` can represent missing values without lossy float coercion.

```python
clean = df.convert_dtypes()
clean['created_at'] = pd.to_datetime(clean['created_at'], errors='coerce', utc=True)
clean['amount'] = pd.to_numeric(clean['amount'], errors='coerce')
clean = clean.dropna(subset=['created_at', 'amount'])
```

## Vectorization

Prefer column operations over Python loops:

```python
df['net'] = df['gross'] - df['discount'].fillna(0)
df['domain'] = df['email'].str.split('@').str[-1].str.lower()
df['month'] = df['created_at'].dt.to_period('M').dt.to_timestamp()
```

Use [[pandas/workflows/transform]] for transformation patterns, [[pandas/workflows/clean]] for dtype repair, and [[pandas/workflows/performance]] when row-wise code is too slow.

sections.title: Series, DataFrame, and Index
sections.body: A `Series` is a one-dimensional labeled array, a `DataFrame` is a collection of aligned Series sharing an index, and an `Index` is the row label object. Arithmetic and assignment align by labels, not only by physical row order. Reset or set indexes intentionally before joins, time-series resampling, or comparison operations.

sections.title: Dtypes and nullable values
sections.body: Dtypes are part of the schema. Object dtype often means mixed Python objects and should be investigated. Prefer `string` for text, `category` for low-cardinality labels, nullable `Int64` for integers with missing values, `boolean` for nullable booleans, and timezone-aware datetimes for external timestamps. Use `convert_dtypes()`, `astype()`, `to_numeric()`, and `to_datetime()` deliberately.

sections.title: Selection and assignment
sections.body: Use `.loc[row_mask, columns]` for label-based selection and assignment. Use `.iloc` only when positions matter. Avoid chained assignment because it can operate on a temporary object. Assign new columns with `df.assign(...)` in method chains or `df.loc[:, 'new_col'] = values` in imperative code.

sections.title: Vectorized operations
sections.body: Vectorized pandas operations run column-wise and delegate work to optimized array code. Use `.str`, `.dt`, arithmetic, boolean masks, `where`, `mask`, `clip`, `map`, `replace`, and `np.select` style patterns before reaching for row loops. If a transformation requires Python logic, isolate it and measure with [[pandas/workflows/performance]].
